title: Why Grocery Formats Keep Shrinking
verdict: Smaller footprints with higher sales density are reshaping what anchor tenancy looks like.
evidence: The average new grocery opening in 2025 occupied roughly 18,000 square feet, down from 45,000 a decade ago. Operators like Trader Joe's, Aldi, and a new generation of specialty formats have demonstrated that constrained assortments and rapid inventory turns can produce sales densities north of $1,200 per square foot. The trend is accelerating in urban infill locations, where smaller formats can absorb spaces that traditional supermarkets would never consider, including former bank branches, ground-floor office conversions, and subdivided big-box retail.
frame: This changes the math on retail leasing in ways that benefit attentive landlords. A grocery tenant that needs 15,000 square feet rather than 50,000 is no longer confined to suburban power centers; it can anchor a mixed-use ground floor or fill a gap in a neighborhood retail corridor. The trade-off is that smaller operators bring less parking demand and more foot traffic, which is exactly the dynamic that elevates surrounding rents. The owners who recognize grocery as a traffic generator rather than a rent generator will capture the premium on adjacent suites.
